title: 2nd Workshop: Challenges and Applications Prospects for Reconfigurable Modular Robots
subtitle: From design concepts to practical deployment: connecting modular robot hardware, planning, control, and learning with real-world applications.
eventDateDisplay: September 27, 2026 (IROS workshop day, pending final conference schedule)
location: IROS 2026, Pittsburgh, PA, USA (room to be announced)
overview: Modular reconfigurable robots (MRRs) offer a promising route toward adaptable robotic systems that can change morphology, workspace, and function to meet task and environmental demands. Recent advances in compliant mechanisms, soft modular structures, self-reconfiguration, task-driven morphology design, and learning-enabled control have expanded the scope of MRRs from laboratory prototypes to emerging real-world applications in construction, inspection, logistics, environmental monitoring, and search-and-rescue. Building on our IROS 2025 workshop on reconfigurable modular robots, this second edition focuses on the challenges and opportunities of taking modular robots from design concepts to practical deployment, bringing together researchers from robot design, control, planning, soft robotics, multi-robot systems, and application domains through invited talks, poster spotlights, live demonstrations, and a moderated panel discussion.

focusAreas.title: Modular Design and Embodiment
focusAreas.description: Versatility and adaptability of modular reconfigurable robots, from compliant mechanisms and soft modular structures to self-reconfiguration.

focusAreas.title: Technological Advancements
focusAreas.description: Novel materials, advanced control systems, and deployment-oriented design that improve modular robot performance and functionality.

focusAreas.title: Real-World Deployment and Benchmarking
focusAreas.description: Reliability, benchmarking, task-driven morphology design, and system integration across different modular robotic paradigms.

focusAreas.title: Industry and Community Collaboration
focusAreas.description: A platform connecting academia, industry, and technology developers to share insights and identify future collaboration opportunities.

cfpIntro: We invite contributions on theory, methods, and systems for modular reconfigurable robots, including both mature deployments and well-scoped early-stage ideas. Extended abstracts, position papers, and poster/demo proposals are all welcome, especially submissions that bring or showcase live modular robot hardware and clearly state assumptions, limitations, and expected real-world impact.
topics: Compliant mechanisms and soft modular structures
Perception and state estimation for modular assemblies
Field deployment: construction, inspection, logistics, environmental monitoring, search-and-rescue

submissionTypes.requirement: 1-page summary plus an optional 2-3 minute video link.
submissionTypes.note: Include hardware setup, floor-space requirements, and safety notes; live modular robot demos are encouraged.
State problem, method, evaluation plan, and limitations.
Identify open technical bottlenecks and concrete community directions for real-world deployment.

reviewCriteria: Relevance to modular reconfigurable robots and workshop scope
Novelty, insight, or practical deployment relevance
Quality of evidence, evaluation design, or system validation
Potential to stimulate cross-community discussion and collaboration

keyDates.label: Submission Site Opens
keyDates.note: Templates, topics, and submission instructions published.

keyDates.label: Submission Deadline
keyDates.note: One month to submit extended abstracts, position papers, and poster/demo proposals.

keyDates.label: Final Materials Due
keyDates.note: Final poster PDFs, demo details, and optional slides.

keyDates.label: Workshop Day
keyDates.note: Final room and schedule synchronized with IROS.

speakers.name: Prof. Mark Yim
speakers.title: Asa Whitney Professor of Mechanical Engineering, Director of the GRASP Lab and ModLab
speakers.bio: A pioneer of self-reconfiguring modular robots, Prof. Yim is a Fellow of the National Academy of Inventors and an IEEE Fellow. He brings senior leadership and long-term perspective to the workshop.

speakers.name: Prof. David Saldana
speakers.title: Associate Professor, Department of Computer Science and Engineering
speakers.bio: Leads SwarmsLab, focusing on coordinating swarms of modular robots and aerial manipulation of flexible objects for real-world tasks. Recipient of an NSF CAREER Award.

speakers.name: Dr. Nikos Tsagarakis
speakers.title: Senior Researcher (Tenured), Principal Investigator, Humanoids and Human Centered Mechatronics (HHCM)
speakers.bio: Leads the HHCM research line at IIT, developing compliant actuators and humanoid and hybrid robotic platforms spanning legged locomotion and autonomous manipulation.

speakers.name: Prof. Sam Kriegman
speakers.title: Assistant Professor of Computer Science, Mechanical Engineering, and Chemical & Biological Engineering
speakers.bio: Known for creating xenobots, the first AI-designed living organisms. AI2050 Fellow and Cozzarelli Prize recipient, researching general theories of embodied life and intelligence.

venueText: The final room assignment, visa-letter process, and travel details will be synchronized with the official IROS 2026 website. The workshop requests tables for speakers with power access, two projectors and screens, a dedicated demo space for modular robot hardware, two additional monitors and desks for live demonstrations, wireless microphones for discussion, and poster boards for roughly 10 posters. Program times are listed in EDT (UTC-4).
